account.title: Your account
account.subtitle: Manage your profile, your data and your privacy.
account.a11yBody: Your text size, easy-read font and high-contrast settings are saved to your account and follow you on every device. Change them any time from the “Reading help” button at the top.
account.dataBody: Download a copy of everything SEQ Elevate holds about you — your progress, badges, Comp Card and reflections — as a file you can keep (GDPR Articles 15 and 20).
account.dangerBody: Permanently delete your account and all your personal data. This cannot be undone. Your facilitator will no longer see your progress or Comp Card.
account.confirmBody: This permanently erases your account, progress, badges and Comp Card. To confirm, type your email address below.
account.deleteError: Something went wrong. Please try again.

mission.workplaceConflictTitle: Try a small but real situation
mission.workplaceConflictHint: 20 minutes. Mobile-friendly. No grades. You can pause.

workplaceConflict.title: Handling a small workplace conflict
workplaceConflict.tagline: 20 minutes · no grades · you can pause anytime

context.title: Three weeks into the new job
context.narrative: You started at a small community café three weeks ago. The team is mostly young people like you. Sam, who started a few weeks before you, has been making sharp remarks about you — sometimes in front of customers. Today Sam said, "Watch out, slow one's on the till." You laughed it off. But your stomach dropped. The shift is still going. What happens next is up to you.
context.anchor: We'll work on this together, step by step.

concept.title: Speaking up without making it worse
concept.body1: Conflict at work is normal. What matters is not avoiding it — it's responding in a way that doesn't escalate the situation, and doesn't shut you down either.
concept.body2: Two small skills carry most of the weight:
concept.point1: Notice what you actually feel, before you react.
concept.point2: Say it clearly, without blaming the other person.
concept.body3: These don't fix every conflict. But they give you a chance to stay in the conversation instead of leaving it — or making it worse.

behaviour.title: What this looks like
behaviour.intro: One useful pattern is the I-statement:
behaviour.formula: When X happens, I feel Y, and I'd like Z.
behaviour.compareLabel: Compare these two responses to Sam:
behaviour.blaming: "You're so rude. Stop being a jerk."
behaviour.iStatement: "When you make comments like that in front of customers, I feel embarrassed. I'd rather we talk to each other directly."
behaviour.closing: Notice the second one doesn't accuse, doesn't escalate, and doesn't back down. It just says what's happening for you.

simulation.title: Try a small move
simulation.prompt: Your colleague says, "Look who finally showed up" when you arrive four minutes late after a delayed bus. You…
simulation.instruction: Pick the response that feels strongest — there's a best answer, but no wrong way to think.

options.blame: "You're always on my case. Get off my back."
options.iStatement: "The bus was late. When you say 'finally', I feel singled out. I'd rather we just say good morning."

feedback.blame: This pushes back hard, which feels good for a moment — but it leaves both of you defensive, and the next shift will be tense.
feedback.shrink: Polite, but you didn't actually say what was true. You'll likely carry the feeling all day.
feedback.iStatement: Strong choice. You named what happened and what you'd prefer, without attacking. That gives the other person a real chance to respond.
feedback.avoid: Sometimes silence is the right move. But here it leaves the comment hanging — and your body will hold it.

scenario.title: The bigger moment
scenario.setup: It's the lunch rush. Sam mutters something about you to a regular customer. The customer laughs awkwardly. You're behind the till.
scenario.question: What do you do?

choices.confront: Tell Sam off in front of the customer
choices.private: Wait for a quiet moment, then talk to Sam privately

outcomes.confront: Sam goes quiet, but you can feel the tension for the rest of the shift. The customer looks uncomfortable. At the end of the day, Sam doesn't say goodbye. The next morning the manager asks what happened.
outcomes.ignore: You feel something hot rise in your chest. By the end of the shift you're exhausted from holding it in. On the bus home you replay the moment.
outcomes.private: You wait 20 minutes. The rush calms. You catch Sam in the back: "Hey — when you said that earlier, I felt put down. Can we talk?" Sam looks surprised: "I didn't mean it like that. I was just messing around. Sorry."
outcomes.manager: The manager listens but says, "You should talk to Sam first." Now you feel both annoyed and stuck.

followup.ignoreTalk: Plan to talk to Sam tomorrow
followup.ignoreShift: Ask the manager for different shifts
followup.privateAccept: Accept the apology and move on
followup.privateAskStop: Accept it, and ask Sam to not do it again
followup.privateBoundary: Tell Sam it's fine, but you'd like to be addressed directly from now on
followup.managerTalk: Go and talk to Sam after all
followup.managerAvoid: Avoid both of them for the rest of the week
followup.managerBack: Go back to the manager and insist

followupOutcomes.confrontExplain: The manager nods. "Next time, try to handle it without making it a scene. But thanks for telling me." It's not closed yet, but it's not lost either.
followupOutcomes.confrontDefensive: The manager looks tired. "I don't want to hear who started it. I want it stopped." You leave feeling smaller.
followupOutcomes.confrontBrush: The manager moves on, but you know it isn't nothing.
followupOutcomes.ignoreTalk: You sleep on it. Tomorrow you find Sam in the break room. The conversation is awkward, but at the end Sam says, "Yeah, I shouldn't have."
followupOutcomes.ignoreSlide: You let it go. It happens again two weeks later. You wonder why you keep ending up here.
followupOutcomes.ignoreShift: The manager says they'll see what they can do. The team notices. It costs you something.
followupOutcomes.privateAccept: The apology lands. The next shift is easier. You feel proud you spoke up.
followupOutcomes.privateAskStop: Sam takes it in. The dynamic shifts. You both work better.
followupOutcomes.privateBoundary: You're clear and not angry. Sam respects it. The next time something starts, Sam catches themselves.
followupOutcomes.managerTalk: You go back to Sam later that day. It's harder than it would have been earlier — but you do it. Sam listens.
followupOutcomes.managerAvoid: By Friday the air is thick. You spend the weekend dreading Monday.
followupOutcomes.managerBack: The manager is firm: "I'm not having this conversation for you." You leave with nothing solved.

reflection.title: Look back
reflection.intro: These prompts are just for you. Nothing here is graded. You can leave any of them blank.
reflection.prompt1: What did you notice in your body during the harder moments?
reflection.prompt2: What was the hardest part of the scenario?
reflection.prompt3: What might you try differently next time?
reflection.placeholder1: It can be a word, a feeling, or a sentence.
reflection.placeholder2: Be honest — only you see this unless you choose to share it.

assessment.title: Quick check
assessment.intro: Three short questions. Not a test — just a way to lock in what you tried.

q1.question: An I-statement does which of the following?
q1.a: Tells the other person what they did wrong
q1.b: Describes what you felt and what you'd prefer

q2.question: What's the first step before responding to a tense moment?

q3.question: Why does waiting for a quiet moment help?
q3.b: It lets both people be heard without an audience
q3.c: It lets the other person forget what they said

scoreEncouragement.all: Nicely done. You've got the pattern.
scoreEncouragement.some: That's a start. Re-read the behaviour model — the rest will click.
scoreEncouragement.none: It happens. Step back, look again, and try the scenario once more. No grade attached.

completion.title: Course complete
completion.body: You walked the full sequence — context, concept, behaviour, practice, scenario, reflection, check. The badge is yours. Your Comp Card is waiting.
completion.badgeMeaning: Spoke up without escalating or shrinking

receivingFeedback.title: Receiving feedback without flinching
receivingFeedback.tagline: 20 minutes · being told something hard, and using it

context.title: The flyer you stayed late for
context.narrative: You're at a work placement at a community centre. They asked you to design a flyer for the youth night. You stayed late, picked the colours, got it just right. This morning you show it to Mara, the coordinator. She looks at it for a few seconds and says, "Hm. This isn't really what I asked for." Your face goes hot. You can feel something drop in your stomach. What happens next is up to you.
context.anchor: We'll take this slowly. Feedback is hard for everyone.

concept.title: Hearing the message, not the threat
concept.body1: When someone criticises your work, your body often reacts as if you're being attacked — heat, a tight chest, the urge to defend or disappear. That's normal. It doesn't mean the feedback is an attack.
concept.body2: Two small skills turn feedback from a wound into something useful:
concept.point1: Separate the work from you. "The flyer missed the point" is not "you are a failure."
concept.point2: Ask one specific question, so you actually know what to change.
concept.body3: You don't have to agree with all of it. But if you can stay in the conversation long enough to understand it, the feedback becomes something you can use instead of something that just hurts.

behaviour.title: What this looks like
behaviour.intro: One useful move is to pause, then ask for the specific thing:
behaviour.formula: Okay — can you show me which part missed, so I can fix it?
behaviour.blaming: "That's not fair. I did exactly what you said."
behaviour.iStatement: "Okay. Can you show me which part missed what you needed?"
behaviour.closing: The second one doesn't collapse and doesn't fight. It treats the feedback as information you can work with — which keeps you in control of what happens next.

simulation.title: Try a small move
simulation.prompt: A teammate reviewing a group report says, "Honestly, your part was kind of confusing." You feel the heat rise. You…
simulation.instruction: Pick the response that feels strongest — there's a best answer, but no wrong way to feel.

options.defend: "It's not confusing, you just read it too fast."
options.crumble: "Ugh, I'm so bad at this, sorry, I'll just redo the whole thing."
options.clarify: "Okay — which part lost you? I'd rather fix the actual spot than guess."

feedback.defend: Pushing back protects you for a second, but now your teammate stops being honest with you — and the confusing part is still confusing.
feedback.crumble: Turning it into 'I'm bad' makes it about your worth, not the work. You'll redo more than you need to and feel worse doing it.
feedback.clarify: Strong choice. You stayed with it and asked for the specific spot. Now you can fix the real problem in five minutes instead of guessing.
feedback.dismiss: Brushing it off ends the discomfort fast — but you lose the one piece of information that would've made the next version better.

scenario.title: The bigger moment
scenario.setup: Back to Mara and the flyer. She's said it "isn't really what she asked for," and she's waiting. Two other staff are within earshot. Your face is still hot.
scenario.question: What do you do?

choices.argue: Explain why you made the choices you made
choices.deflate: Apologise a lot and go quiet
choices.askWhat: Ask what specifically missed the mark
choices.takeTime: Say you'll take another look and come back

outcomes.argue: You start explaining the colours, the font, your reasoning. Mara's face closes a little. "I hear you, but I don't have time to debate it — it just needs to match the brief." You feel both defensive and stuck.
outcomes.deflate: "Sorry, sorry, I'll redo it, sorry." Mara softens but looks a bit awkward. The other staff glance over. You've ended the moment, but you still have no idea what was actually wrong.
outcomes.askWhat: You breathe. "Okay — can you show me which part missed what you needed?" Mara points: "The date's buried, and it reads more like a party than a support night." Oh. That's… actually clear. And fixable.
outcomes.takeTime: "Let me take another look and come back to you." Mara nods. "Sure." You've bought yourself space — but you'll still need to find out what she meant.

followup.argueDouble: Keep explaining — you want her to get it
followup.argueConcede: Stop, and ask what she'd change
followup.argueAsk: Let it go and ask for the brief again
followup.deflateSpiral: Go home and feel terrible about it
followup.deflateRegroup: Next morning, ask Mara to walk you through the brief
followup.deflateAsk: Message a colleague to check what Mara meant
followup.askFix: Fix the two things she named and show her again
followup.askDefend: Fix the date, but keep your design as is
followup.askOverwhelm: Try to change everything at once
followup.takeReturn: Re-read the brief, then go back with questions
followup.takeAvoid: Quietly hope she forgets about it
followup.takeRush: Redo it fast without checking what she meant

followupOutcomes.argueDouble: The more you explain, the more it sounds like excuses — to her and to you. The moment sours. Nothing about the flyer is fixed.
followupOutcomes.argueConcede: "Okay, I'll stop — what would you change?" Mara exhales. "Just the date and the tone." You got there, a little bruised, but you got there.
followupOutcomes.argueAsk: "Can I see the brief again?" She sends it. Reading it now, calmer, you can see what she meant. Not lost.
followupOutcomes.deflateSpiral: You replay it all evening. By morning the flyer still isn't fixed and you feel smaller about a thing that was never that big.
followupOutcomes.deflateRegroup: You sleep on it. In the morning: "Can you walk me through the brief?" Mara does, kindly. The fix takes ten minutes. You feel the difference between flinching and following up.
followupOutcomes.deflateAsk: A colleague tells you what Mara usually means by 'on brief.' It helps — though hearing it from Mara herself would've helped more.
followupOutcomes.askFix: You fix the buried date and the party tone. You show Mara. "Yes — that's it." Something settles in you. You took the hit and used it.
followupOutcomes.askDefend: You fix the date but keep the party look. Mara half-nods. "Closer." Part of the feedback landed; part didn't. Workable.
followupOutcomes.askOverwhelm: You try to redo everything and tie yourself in knots. The new version is different but not better. Sometimes 'fix two things' beats 'fix everything.'
followupOutcomes.takeReturn: You re-read the brief, jot two questions, and go back. Mara answers them in a minute. The second flyer is right. The pause worked because you used it.
followupOutcomes.takeAvoid: You hope it blows over. Two days later Mara asks for it. Now it's late and still wrong, and the gap got bigger.
followupOutcomes.takeRush: You rush a new version without checking what she meant. It misses again — differently. The pause only helps if you use it to find out more.

reflection.title: Look back
reflection.intro: These prompts are just for you. Nothing here is graded. Leave any of them blank.
reflection.prompt1: Where did you feel the feedback in your body?
reflection.prompt2: What's the difference between 'the work missed' and 'I'm a failure'?
reflection.prompt3: What's one question you could ask next time, before reacting?
reflection.placeholder1: Heat, stomach, jaw, hands — wherever it showed up.
reflection.placeholder2: In your own words. Only you see this.
reflection.placeholder3: Even a single sentence you could keep in your pocket.

assessment.title: Quick check
assessment.intro: Three short questions. Not a test — just a way to lock in what you tried.

q1.question: When feedback feels like an attack, the useful first step is to…
q1.b: Separate the work from your worth, then listen
q1.c: Agree with everything to end it

q2.question: Why ask 'which part specifically?'
q2.b: So you know the real thing to change instead of guessing

q3.question: Taking time before responding helps only if you…
q3.b: Use the pause to find out what was actually meant

scoreEncouragement.all: Nicely done. You've got the pattern.
scoreEncouragement.some: That's a start. Re-read the behaviour model — the rest will click.
scoreEncouragement.none: It happens. Step back, look again, and try the scenario once more. No grade attached.

completion.title: Course complete
completion.body: You walked the full sequence — context, concept, behaviour, practice, scenario, reflection, check. Taking feedback without breaking on it is a real skill, and you just practised it. Your Comp Card is waiting.
completion.badgeMeaning: Took hard feedback and used it instead of breaking on it
